title: The First AI Winter
concept: An 'AI Winter' is a period when funding and interest in AI research collapse; the first one, triggered partly by the perceptron's proven limits, lasted from the early 1970s into the 1980s.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What did Minsky and Papert's 1969 book prove about single-layer perceptrons?

options.text: They can't learn non-linearly-separable patterns like XOR
They can learn any pattern given enough time

exercises.explain: Right — a rigorous proof, not just an opinion.

cards.title: When the funding dries up
cards.body: Imagine a hot new toy every kid wants — then, almost overnight, stores stop stocking it and companies stop making it. Something similar happened to AI research in the 1970s. Money, jobs, and headlines about neural networks all but vanished for over a decade.

cards.title: What is an 'AI Winter'?
cards.body: An AI Winter is a period when enthusiasm, funding, and research output for artificial intelligence sharply collapse after a wave of unmet hype. Governments and companies pull their money out, labs shut down, and researchers move to other fields.

cards.title: A garden after a frost
cards.body: AI research in the late 1950s and 60s was like a garden in full bloom — lots of funding, lots of bold promises. Then a hard frost hit: unmet expectations (like the XOR problem) killed the growth. The garden didn't disappear, but almost nothing new grew there for years.

cards.title: The timeline
cards.body: The first AI Winter stretched from the early 1970s into the 1980s. Major government reports in the US and UK concluded AI research had overpromised and underdelivered. Agencies like DARPA slashed funding, and "artificial intelligence" became a phrase some researchers avoided in grant proposals.

cards.title: The perceptron's limits weren't the ONLY cause
cards.body: It's tempting to blame the whole AI Winter on the XOR problem alone. In reality, it was one big contributor among several: computers were also too slow and small for the era's ambitions, and multiple bold predictions about AI (like near-term human-level translation) simply didn't come true on schedule.

cards.title: AI research: before vs. during the winter

left.text:
Big funding, big headlines
"Thinking machines" hyped everywhere

right.text:
Funding cuts, closed labs
"AI" becomes an unpopular word

options.text: A period when AI funding and research enthusiasm sharply collapse
A season when computers run slower due to heat
A type of neural network architecture

exercises.explain: An AI Winter is about collapsing confidence and funding, not the weather or a conference!

exercises.prompt: True or false: The first AI Winter lasted from the early 1970s into the 1980s.
exercises.explain: True — roughly a decade-plus of reduced funding and interest followed the unmet promises and proven limits of early AI systems.

exercises.sentence: An AI Winter happens when research funding and public ___ in AI sharply collapse.
exercises.explain: It's a collapse of confidence (and the funding that follows it), not a technical event.

exercises.prompt: Which of these was a real contributor to the first AI Winter?

options.text: Government reports concluding AI had overpromised and underdelivered
A new law banning neural network research

exercises.explain: Critical government reports (like the US and UK reviews) directly led to major funding cuts.

exercises.prompt: A funding agency in 1972 reads Minsky and Papert's proof about the perceptron's limits, alongside slower-than-promised progress in other AI projects. They decide to cut AI research budgets sharply. A few years later, this pattern repeats across multiple agencies and countries. What term describes the resulting era, and why did it last so long?

options.text: An AI Winter — it lasted years because rebuilding trust and funding takes far longer than losing it, especially without new breakthroughs to reverse the narrative
An AI Renaissance — new investment kept pouring in
A Perceptron Boom — perceptron sales spiked
Nothing changed — funding stayed the same

exercises.explain: Exactly. Trust is slow to rebuild. But history has a twist: the seed of a fix for XOR was hiding in an idea researchers had already brushed past. Next up: the clue everyone almost missed.
